summary:
The Durrani Empire (1747–1823 AD), founded by Ahmad Shah Durrani (Ahmad Shah Abdali), was the largest Afghan empire in history and the immediate predecessor of modern Afghanistan, created when Ahmad Shah was chosen as the first king of a unified Afghanistan in 1747 after the assassination of [[event:2447|Nader Shah]] left a power vacuum across Central Asia. Ahmad Shah, an ethnic Pashtun who had served as a commander in Nader Shah's army, was selected by a traditional Pashtun tribal assembly (jirga) to lead the newly unified Durrani (Abdali) Pashtun confederacy. Within two decades, he had created an empire stretching from eastern Persia to the Punjab, incorporating Kashmir, Sindh, and parts of the Mughal heartland.

Ahmad Shah's most historically significant campaigns were his nine invasions of India. His victory at the [[event:2448|Third Battle of Panipat]] (1761) against the Maratha Confederacy halted Maratha expansion into the Punjab and northern India — though Ahmad Shah proved more interested in raiding for treasure than in building a permanent Indian empire. His campaigns demonstrated both Afghan military power (the Pashtun cavalry and Afghan infantry were formidable) and the structural limits of tribal confederacies: the Durrani Empire lacked the administrative infrastructure to hold its vast territories without the personal military presence of a capable ruler. After Ahmad Shah's death in 1772, the empire fragmented through succession disputes. The Durrani state's legacy was the crystallization of an Afghan national identity around the Durrani Pashtun tribal confederacy, and the establishment of Kandahar and later Kabul as political centers of a recognizably Afghan state — the foundation of the modern nation.
